content.text: Domain stewardship for agentic AI knowledge bases is the practice of assigning named, accountable individuals — domain stewards — to own, curate, and certify the data assets that feed AI retrieval systems. Each steward is responsible for one logical domain (Finance, HR, Legal, Product, etc.) and acts as the authoritative decision-maker for which documents, records, and data products from that domain are eligible to be embedded into a vector index used by AI agents. The role bridges the technical team that builds and operates the retrieval pipeline and the business units that produce and consume the knowledge.

Agentic AI systems retrieve and synthesise information at runtime rather than relying solely on parametric knowledge baked into model weights. The quality, accuracy, and access-appropriateness of retrieved content therefore determine the quality of every agent response. Without domain stewardship, knowledge bases accumulate stale documents, superseded policies, and conflicting versions from different departments — and agents surface all of them indiscriminately.

Stewardship also enforces a human accountability chain. When an agent cites an incorrect HR policy or an outdated contract clause, there must be a named individual who owns that error path, can correct the source document, and can trace when the bad content entered the index. Without stewards, accountability diffuses across teams and corrections stall.
— agents retrieve only content that a domain expert has certified as current and accurate, reducing hallucination risk from stale retrieval context.
— every document in the knowledge base has a named owner who can be contacted for corrections, updates, or access decisions.
— when a problematic retrieval is reported, the steward can remove or replace the offending document without involving engineering, reducing mean time to resolution.
— stewardship records demonstrate to auditors that AI outputs are grounded in approved, reviewed sources, supporting AI transparency obligations under frameworks like the EU AI Act.
— domain stewards define which documents are retrievable by which agent roles or user personas, preventing confidential data from surfacing in public-facing agents.
— certification is a manual review process. A large domain with thousands of documents can overwhelm a single steward; steward capacity must be scoped to the volume.
— business units may resist the additional process overhead of stewardship reviews, especially if they are not direct consumers of the AI system.
— if review cadences are infrequent, newly published documents can sit uncertified for weeks, limiting the freshness of the knowledge base.
— when a steward leaves the organisation without a designated successor, their domain's documents lose their accountable owner; knowledge base quality can degrade silently.
— cross-domain documents (a procurement policy that touches both Finance and Legal) require clear ownership rules to avoid duplication or ownership gaps.

A domain steward programme operates through five repeating activities. First, domain scoping maps each logical data domain to a responsible steward and a deputy. Second, source cataloguing identifies which systems, document repositories, and data products are in scope for each domain's knowledge base. Third, certification review requires the steward to approve documents before they enter the embedding pipeline — either individually for high-sensitivity content or batch-approved for low-sensitivity bulk imports. Fourth, freshness monitoring tracks the age of certified documents against a staleness threshold (typically 90 days for policies, 30 days for operational data) and alerts the steward when re-review is due. Fifth, incident response gives stewards the authority to trigger immediate removal of a document from the vector index when a retrieval error is reported.

The technical implementation embeds a 'steward-approved' metadata flag and a certification timestamp into every document's vector store record. At retrieval time, the search pipeline filters to only return chunks whose certification flag is set to true and whose certification timestamp is within the staleness window. Documents that fail either check are excluded from retrieval context until re-certified.

attrs.alt: Domain stewardship architecture: four domain stewards own domain knowledge bases that feed into a central RAG vector index consumed by an agentic AI system at query time.

content.text: Each business domain has a designated steward who governs which content enters that domain's knowledge base. The domain KBs feed a shared RAG vector index through an embedding pipeline that enforces certification metadata. At query time, the agentic AI retrieves only certified, non-stale chunks. Documents not yet certified by a steward remain in a staging area, visible to stewards for review but excluded from production retrieval.
When agentic AI responses are grounded in internal knowledge that changes frequently (policies, procedures, org structures) and where an outdated answer has business or legal consequences.
When the knowledge base spans multiple business domains with different sensitivity levels and different subject-matter experts.
When regulatory or audit requirements demand an accountable human reviewer for AI grounding data.
When the organisation has experienced AI responses citing superseded documents and needs a governance control to prevent recurrence.

— map each major business function to a logical domain. Use the data product catalogue as a starting point. Avoid domains that are too broad (one steward owns everything) or too narrow (steward overhead exceeds benefit).
— each domain requires a primary steward and at least one deputy. Deputies prevent single points of failure when the primary is on leave or leaves the organisation.

— a simple web UI or ticketing workflow that lets stewards see pending documents, approve or reject them, set a re-review date, and flag urgent removals. A Notion database, Jira board, or purpose-built data catalogue (Collibra, DataHub) can serve this function.
Embed certification metadata in the vector store
— every document chunk must carry 'steward_approved: true/false', 'certified_by', 'certified_at', and 'expires_at' fields in its metadata payload.
— add a metadata filter to the vector search query that restricts results to approved, non-expired chunks.
— a scheduled job checks all certified documents daily and sends email or Slack notifications to the steward when a document is within 14 days of its expiry.

# stewardship/certification_filter.py
# Apply at retrieval time to restrict to steward-certified chunks

from datetime import datetime, timezone
from typing import Any

def build_certification_filter(domain: str | None = None) -> dict[str, Any]:
    """
    Returns a Weaviate / Qdrant / pgvector metadata filter
    that includes only steward-approved, non-expired chunks.
    """
    now_iso = datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat()
    base_filter = {
        "must": [
            {"key": "steward_approved", "match": {"value": True}},
            {"key": "expires_at", "range": {"gt": now_iso}},
        ]
    }
    if domain:
        base_filter["must"].append(
            {"key": "domain", "match": {"value": domain}}
        )
    return base_filter


# Example usage with a Qdrant client
from qdrant_client import QdrantClient
from qdrant_client.models import Filter, FieldCondition, MatchValue, Range

def search_certified(query_vector: list[float], domain: str, top_k: int = 8):
    client = QdrantClient(url="http://localhost:6333")
    now = datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat()

    results = client.search(
        collection_name="enterprise_kb",
        query_vector=query_vector,
        query_filter=Filter(
            must=[
                FieldCondition(key="steward_approved", match=MatchValue(value=True)),
                FieldCondition(key="domain", match=MatchValue(value=domain)),
                FieldCondition(key="expires_at", range=Range(gt=now)),
            ]
        ),
        limit=top_k,
    )
    return results

# stewardship/staleness_alert.py
# Daily job: alert stewards about documents nearing expiry

from datetime import datetime, timedelta, timezone
from dataclasses import dataclass
import smtplib
from email.message import EmailMessage

@dataclass
class DocumentRecord:
    doc_id: str
    title: str
    domain: str
    steward_email: str
    expires_at: datetime

def check_expiring_documents(
    documents: list[DocumentRecord],
    warning_days: int = 14,
) -> list[DocumentRecord]:
    threshold = datetime.now(timezone.utc) + timedelta(days=warning_days)
    return [
        doc for doc in documents
        if doc.expires_at <= threshold
    ]

def send_steward_alert(doc: DocumentRecord, smtp_host: str) -> None:
    msg = EmailMessage()
    msg["Subject"] = f"[KB Stewardship] Review required: {doc.title}"
    msg["From"] = "kb-governance@company.com"
    msg["To"] = doc.steward_email
    msg.set_content(
        f"Document '{doc.title}' (domain: {doc.domain}) expires on "
        f"{doc.expires_at.date()}. Please review and re-certify or remove it "
        f"from the knowledge base.\n\nReview portal: https://kb-portal/review/{doc.doc_id}"
    )
    with smtplib.SMTP(smtp_host) as s:
        s.send_message(msg)

A global professional services firm deployed an internal AI assistant that answered employee questions about HR policies, IT procedures, and project governance. Initially the knowledge base was populated by bulk-importing every document from the corporate intranet — over 18,000 files spanning a decade of content. Within three weeks, employees reported the assistant citing a parental leave policy that had been superseded two years earlier and a data residency procedure that applied to a region the firm had exited.

The firm introduced domain stewardship as a corrective control. HR, Legal, Finance, IT, and Project Management each nominated a steward and a deputy. A certification portal was built as a Jira Service Management queue — documents awaiting approval appeared as tickets, and stewards processed them within a 5-business-day SLA. Each approved document received a 90-day certification window, after which the steward received an alert and the document was automatically suspended from retrieval until re-reviewed.

After three months, the certified knowledge base contained 2,100 documents — 88% fewer than the original bulk import — but the accuracy of AI responses on policy questions increased from 61% to 94% by internal user survey. The reduction in scope was not a failure; it was the stewards correctly identifying that the remaining 15,900 documents were outdated, duplicated, or simply not useful for grounding AI responses.
Assigning stewardship to a team rather than a named individual
— team ownership diffuses accountability. A certification queue assigned to 'the HR team' typically goes unprocessed; a queue assigned to a named steward does not.
— the fastest way to populate a knowledge base is also the fastest way to contaminate it with stale content. Always start with a certified subset.
— a 365-day certification window for HR policies in a company with frequent policy changes means outdated content can persist for nearly a year.
— when the primary steward is unavailable, certification queues stall and newly updated documents remain excluded from retrieval.

— a vendor contract touches both Legal and Finance. Without explicit rules for cross-domain ownership, these documents fall into gaps and either are certified twice with conflicting metadata or not at all.

— HR policies may warrant 90-day re-review; financial reports may require 30 days; IT runbooks may be stable enough for 180 days. Tailor the expiry window to the rate of change of each domain's content.
— connect the steward portal to the embedding pipeline so that an approval in the portal triggers automatic ingestion without a manual engineering step.
— record who approved or rejected each document, when, and any comments. This log is the audit trail that demonstrates governance to regulators.

— review the certification queue age, the fraction of the knowledge base nearing expiry, and the steward's mean time to process requests. Use these metrics to identify domains that need more steward capacity.
Give stewards tooling to search the existing index
— before certifying a new document, a steward should be able to check whether a newer version already exists in the index, preventing duplicate and conflicting content.

The stewardship workflow is the rate-limiting step in knowledge base population, not the embedding pipeline. The key scalability lever is certification throughput — the number of documents a steward can review per week. For bulk imports, batch-certification tools that present multiple documents of the same type (e.g., all Q1 expense policy PDFs) in a single review session can increase throughput tenfold compared to document-by-document review.

For large domains, a tiered model works well: the domain steward sets category-level approval rules ('all documents tagged finance/approved-vendor are auto-certified for 30 days') and only reviews exceptions or high-sensitivity items individually. Automated pre-screening using an LLM to flag potentially sensitive or confidential content reduces the steward's review burden by surfacing only the documents that require human judgment.
Steward portal, certification workflows, audit trail
Enterprise-grade; integrates with major data catalogues
Open-source; strong REST API for pipeline integration
Lowest adoption friction for orgs already using Jira
Vector store with metadata filter support
All support payload/metadata filters at query time
Small KBs with a dedicated content ops team

Stewardship controls directly reduce the attack surface of retrieval-augmented generation. If an attacker can inject a malicious document into the knowledge base, it will be retrieved and surfaced to users — a prompt injection at the data layer. The certification workflow acts as a content integrity gate: only steward-approved documents enter the index, and any document modification after certification should invalidate its approved status and require re-review.
— store a SHA-256 hash of each document at certification time. Verify the hash before serving a chunk; a hash mismatch triggers automatic suspension and a steward alert.
— the certification portal must require MFA. A compromised steward account can approve malicious documents, so strong authentication is a hard requirement.
— certification decisions must be append-only; no update or delete. An adversary who gains write access to the audit log could backdate approvals.

Domain stewardship assigns a named, accountable human to certify the accuracy and appropriateness of AI grounding data — transforming knowledge base governance from a shared responsibility into a traceable one.
The core technical mechanism is a certification metadata filter applied at retrieval time: agents only receive chunks whose steward_approved flag is true and whose expiry has not passed.

Stewardship is the rate-limiting step in KB population; batch certification tools and tiered approval rules are the primary levers for scaling throughput without proportionally scaling steward headcount.
A deputy steward is not optional — it is a reliability control. Single-steward domains create availability risk when the primary is unavailable.
Content hashing at certification time and hash verification at retrieval time prevent post-certification tampering, closing a data-layer prompt injection risk.
In practice, thorough stewardship typically reduces knowledge base size significantly while improving response accuracy — quality of grounding content matters far more than volume.
